title: Status of the world's fish stocks
subtitle: [Fish stocks](#dod:fish-stock) become overexploited when fish are caught at a rate higher than the population can support, and the ability of the stock to produce its [Maximum Sustainable Yield (MSY)](#dod:msy) is jeopardized.
note: Only data on assessed fish stocks are included here; many fish stocks across Africa, Asia, and South America have not been assessed.
caption:
Status of the world's fish stocks

Fish stocks become overexploited when fish are caught at a rate higher than the population can support, and the ability of the stock to produce its Maximum Sustainable Yield (MSY) is jeopardized.

This stacked area chart displays the percentage of assessed fish stocks categorized as either biologically sustainable or overexploited from 1974 to 2021. The vertical axis ranges from 0% to 100%, while the horizontal axis marks years at intervals including 1974, 1980, 1990, 2000, 2010, and 2021. The upper blue area represents biologically sustainable stocks, and the lower red area represents overexploited stocks. In 1974, the red area occupies a small portion at the bottom, sitting below the 10% mark, leaving the vast majority of the chart covered in blue. Through the 1980s, the red area expands, crossing the 10% line and reaching near 20% by 1990. A sharp dip occurs around 1990, dropping the red area back toward 15%, followed by a gradual rise through the 1990s and 2000s. By 2010, the red area sits between 20% and 30%. In 2021, the final year shown, the red area reaches its highest point on the chart, sitting just below the 40% line. Consequently, the blue area, which dominates the chart throughout the timeline, contracts from nearly 90% in 1974 to approximately 60% in 2021. The data includes only assessed fish stocks, noting that many stocks across Africa, Asia, and South America have not been assessed.
